title: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28(2) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.28: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.28' running on CIM Network_Traffic data model, index=proxy, gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28(4) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28(4) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.28: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.28' running on CIM Network_Traffic data model, index=proxy, gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as src_count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-7d All_Traffic.action="allowed"
  by All_Traffic.dest
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| eval dest_domain=replace(dest,"^\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$","")
| lookup gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv dest_domain OUTPUT primary_processor, approval_status
| where isnotnull(primary_processor) AND (approval_status!="approved" OR isnull(approval_status))
| sort - bytes_out
| table dest, dest_domain, primary_processor, approval_status, bytes_out, src_count
description: Processors must not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. This use case detects new destination domains, SaaS tenants, or infrastructure regions receiving personal data from approved processors — flagging unapproved sub-processing paths inferred from network and SaaS audit telemetry.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes proxy. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Build allowlist of approved processor and sub-processor domains from DPAs; (2) classify egress traffic by processor parent using DNS and TLS SNI metadata; (3) alert on high-volume flows to unlisted destinations; (4) require procurement approval workflow to update allowlist; (5) document investigations for Art. 28 accountability. Indexes required: `index=proxy`; Lookups: `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist`, `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv`.
visualization: Table (unapproved destinations), Sankey (flow from segment to destination), Single value (new destinations this week), Bar chart (bytes by processor).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: PII detections from authorized data exports, finance reports, support cases with logged PII, or anonymized data testing.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CIM Network_Traffic data model, `index=proxy`, `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Build allowlist of approved processor and sub-processor domains from DPAs; (2) classify egress traffic by processor parent using DNS and TLS SNI metadata; (3) alert on high-volume flows to unlisted destinations; (4) require procurement approval workflow to update allowlist; (5) document investigations for Art. 28 accountability.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as src_count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-7d All_Traffic.action="allowed"
  by All_Traffic.dest
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| eval dest_domain=replace(dest,"^\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$","")
| lookup gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv dest_domain OUTPUT primary_processor, approval_status
| where isnotnull(primary_processor) AND (approval_status!="approved" OR isnull(approval_status))
| sort - bytes_out
| table dest, dest_domain, primary_processor, approval_status, bytes_out, src_count
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring (Art. 28(2), Art. 28(4))** — Processors must not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. This use case detects new destination domains, SaaS tenants, or infrastructure regions receiving personal data from approved processors — flagging unapproved sub-processing paths inferred from network and SaaS audit telemetry.

Documented **Data sources**: CIM Network_Traffic data model, `index=proxy`, `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **dest_domain** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(primary_processor) AND (approval_status!="approved" OR isnull(approval_status))` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: approval_status, bytes_out, dest, dest_domain, primary_processor, src_count.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unapproved destinations), Sankey (flow from segment to destination), Single value (new destinations this week), Bar chart (bytes by processor).
grandmaExplanation: We processors must not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
